title: Today's AI Brief: TSMC struggles to keep up with AI demand
summary: TSMC struggles with AI demand, ChatGPT gets a memory boost
imageAlt: Image from theverge.com for TSMC struggles to keep up with AI demand: ‘We can only support so much’
imageCaption: TSMC struggles to keep up with AI demand: ‘We can only support so much’ (theverge.com)

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to your AI briefing. Let's dive into the latest news. The world's largest chipmaker, TSMC, is facing a challenge: keeping up with the surging demand for AI chips. In a recent statement, TSMC's leadership acknowledged that fulfilling customer needs, especially with their new US-based production, might take a very long time. This is a big deal because TSMC is a key player in the AI hardware ecosystem, supplying chips to major tech companies.

TSMC's capacity issues highlight the intense demand for AI infrastructure. As AI models grow more complex, they require more powerful chips. TSMC's current production limits mean that customers might have to wait longer for the hardware they need. It's like when your favorite coffee shop can't keep up with demand, and you have to wait in line a bit longer – except here, the coffee is AI compute power.

On a more exciting note, OpenAI has announced a new memory feature for ChatGPT. This update allows ChatGPT to retain some context from previous conversations, making interactions feel more natural and less repetitive. For instance, if you're discussing a project with ChatGPT over several sessions, it can now recall your preferences and pick up where you left off. This is a significant step towards making AI assistants more helpful and less frustrating to use.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.
